messages.content:
\subsubsection{Timely Interactive Games}
We first wrap Jericho into an environment that an LLM can directly interact with.
Jericho\footnote{\url{https://github.com/microsoft/jericho}} offers interfaces that connect learning agents to interactive fiction games (e.g., \textit{Zork}).
In each game, models can interact with the game environment for multiple rounds, and choose from actions to obtain rewards.
For each model, we first test its speed and then use an integer multiple of the average per-step latency as the time budget.
Since these games are lightweight, environment responses are typically fast; thus, models are first encouraged to spend the budget on more interaction rounds.
To further evaluate reasoning under tool-dominated latency, we additionally inject extra interaction delay, testing whether the model can explore more effectively under tighter time constraints.
\subsubsection{Timely Machine Learning}
Evaluation on machine learning tasks require the model to train an ML model to solve a given problem.
We select several tasks from MLEBench-Lite~\cite{mlebench}.
The model can use code interpreter to run code and produce a submission file, which is then scored.
During interaction, the model observes execution outputs, error messages, and the current score, and iteratively improves its solution.
After speed benchmarking, we estimate the average per-round time $\tau$, which includes both LLM reasoning and model training, and allocate an integer multiple of $\tau$ as the budget.
Because training is typically time-consuming, this suite evaluates whether the model can make effective use of additional time budget when tool cost dominates.
\subsubsection{Timely General Reasoning}
Finally, we return to standard reasoning tasks, where test-time is largely equivalent to generation time.
To ensure the model conditions on \emph{time} (rather than only token length), we provide a timer tool that reports elapsed time; the model must query it during reasoning to avoid timing out.
As above, we benchmark the model to obtain an average latency $\tau$, and set the per-instance time budget as a scaled multiple of $\tau$.
This suite tests (i) whether time awareness emerges naturally, and (ii) whether test-time scaling observed under token budgets still holds under explicit time constraints. 这一段翻译自我的原文: Timely-Eval
接下来，为了衡量模型在agentic任务上，对时间这一变量的awareness和调节能力，我们设计了Timely Eval这一benchmark。Timely-Eval包含了三类任务: (i) Text Games, (ii) Machine Learning Tasks and (iii) General Reasoning Tasks. 下面我们将逐个介绍这三类任务的评测设计。
1.  Timely Interactive Games
    
我们首先将Jericho这一文字游戏的交互环境搭建成了LLM可调用的文字游戏环境。Jericho是一个python-based interface connecting learning agents with interactive fiction games. 其中包含了一些经典的text-based fiction games，如zork。在这个环境中，模型被允许和环境进行多轮的交互，通过抉择不同的行为来获取潜在收益。
我们对一个模型服务首先进行速度测试，并将单步的平均时延乘以整数倍作为模型的时间开销限制。由于这些text game都比较轻量，因此在这些任务上的真实交互时间是更加迅速的。因而，在Interactive Games中，模型首先被鼓励进行更多轮的交互以获取收益，而不是将更多的时间放在推理上。而为了继续评测环境占主导情况下的推理，我们将交互时间增加，以此评测模型能否在更复杂的环境下，在有限的时间内进行更有效的探索。
2.  Timely Machine Learning
    
接下来，Machine Learning Tasks要求模型针对一个Machine Learning任务训练一个对应的机器学习模型来解决问题。我们从MLEBench-Lite中挑选了几个机器学习任务作为评测的基础。在这些任务中，模型被允许使用code解释器来执行代码并生成submission文件，这个文件接下来会被计算对应的得分，模型可以获取到代码执行的输出，错误信息，以及最终模型的得分，并进行多轮的交互以提升模型性能。对一个模型服务，我们在进行速度测试后，得到了单轮LLM推理和ML模型训练的平均时长τ，模型会接下来被给予整数倍的τ作为更大的时间开销。由于训练机器学习模型的时间一般较长，因此Machine Learning Tasks旨在评测模型在工具调用相对主导的环境下，是否能正确利用更多的time budget。
3.  Timely General Reasoning
    
在第三部分，我们将视角放回通用的推理任务上。在这些任务上，test time和generation time是等价的。为了确保模型是根据时间开销进行推理的，我们为模型提供了一个计时器工具以获取经过的时间，模型需要在推理中调用计时器以防止自己超时。
在这一任务上，我们仍然是首先进行速度测试，并接下来对平均时延τ乘以一定的系数作为单条样本的用时预算。在general task中，我们想验证的是模型对于time的感知是否是天然存在的，以及模型在token预算上表现出的test time scaling能力是否在time限制下仍然作用。 我认为有点太平淡了？有没有修改意见？请确保流畅和清晰。

我理解你的顾虑。现有版本确实有些平铺直叙。让我提供一个更有层次、更引人入胜的版本：

```latex
\subsubsection{Timely Interactive Games}

Interactive fiction games present a natural testbed where \emph{interaction speed} often outweighs \emph{reasoning depth}. 
We build upon Jericho\footnote{\url{https://github.com/microsoft/jericho}}, a Python interface connecting agents to classic text-based games like \textit{Zork}.
In these environments, models navigate through multiple rounds of interaction, selecting actions to maximize rewards.

The lightweight nature of these games creates an interesting challenge: environment responses are nearly instantaneous, so the bottleneck shifts entirely to the model itself.
We first benchmark each model to obtain its average per-step latency $\tau$, then set the time budget as an integer multiple of $\tau$.
This design \emph{incentivizes exploration over deliberation}---models must balance the trade-off between thinking longer per step versus taking more steps overall.
To push this further, we introduce artificial interaction delays, simulating scenarios where tool latency dominates and forcing models to explore more strategically under tighter constraints.

\subsubsection{Timely Machine Learning}

Machine learning tasks flip the script: here, \emph{tool execution} dominates the time budget.
We adapt several tasks from MLEBench-Lite~\cite{mlebench}, where models must train ML models to solve given problems.
Models interact with a code interpreter, submitting solutions and observing execution outputs, error messages, and performance scores across multiple iterations.

After speed benchmarking to estimate the average per-round latency $\tau$ (including both LLM reasoning and model training), we allocate multiples of $\tau$ as the time budget.
Since training often consumes orders of magnitude more time than reasoning, this suite evaluates a different skill: \emph{can models make judicious use of extra time when tool costs dominate?}
The key question is whether models learn to invest time wisely---running fewer, better-designed experiments rather than exhaustive trial-and-error.

\subsubsection{Timely General Reasoning}

Finally, we return to pure reasoning tasks, where test-time equals generation time.
This setup poses a subtle challenge: how do we ensure models condition on \emph{actual elapsed time} rather than just token counts?
We introduce a timer tool that models must explicitly query to track progress and avoid timeouts.
As before, we benchmark to obtain $\tau$ and set per-instance budgets as scaled multiples.

This suite addresses two questions: (i) Does time awareness emerge naturally when models have explicit access to timing information? (ii) Does the test-time scaling observed under token budgets transfer to explicit time constraints?
Put differently, we ask whether models can develop a genuine sense of urgency---not just generating longer responses, but reasoning more effectively within fixed time horizons.
```

**主要改进：**

1. **更强的开头**：每个小节用一句话点明核心特点（"interaction speed outweighs reasoning depth" / "tool execution dominates" / "test-time equals generation time"）

2. **递进关系**：三个任务形成对比 - 交互主导 → 工具主导 → 纯推理

3. **生动的表达**：
   - "flip the script"
   - "incentivizes exploration over deliberation"
   - "genuine sense of urgency"

4. **明确动机**：每个任务都清楚说明要测试什么能力

5. **关键问题突出**：用斜体强调核心trade-off和设计目的

觉得这个版本如何？
帮我先简单写个论文摘要。

```latex
\begin{abstract}
As large language models (LLMs) increasingly power real-world applications, understanding their behavior under \emph{time constraints} becomes critical.
Existing work on test-time scaling focuses on token budgets or computational steps, overlooking a fundamental reality: in production systems, models face hard \emph{wall-clock time limits}.
This paper asks: \textbf{can LLMs effectively manage time when it becomes a first-class constraint?}

We introduce \textsc{Timely-Eval}, a benchmark designed to evaluate time-aware reasoning across three diverse settings: interactive games where rapid exploration matters, machine learning tasks where tool execution dominates latency, and general reasoning problems where thinking time equals wall-clock time.
Unlike token-based evaluations, our benchmark explicitly tracks elapsed time and requires models to balance reasoning depth against time budgets through tool-mediated time awareness.

Through extensive experiments, we uncover a striking gap: while frontier models demonstrate strong test-time scaling under token budgets, their performance degrades significantly when forced to reason under explicit time constraints.
Models struggle to develop time awareness even when given access to timer tools, often either timing out or underutilizing available time.
We further show that this gap persists across task types---whether time is spent on reasoning, tool calls, or exploration.

Our findings reveal that \textbf{time-aware reasoning remains an open challenge}, distinct from existing notions of test-time compute scaling.
We release \textsc{Timely-Eval} to facilitate future research on building models that can truly reason under pressure.
\end{abstract}
```

**摘要结构：**
1. **动机**（2句）：为什么时间约束重要，现有工作的gap
2. **核心问题**（1句）：用粗体提出关键问题
3. **方法**（2句）：介绍Timely-Eval的设计
4. **发现**（3句）：主要实验结果
5. **结论**（2句）：总结意义和贡献

这个摘要大约200词，突出了三个关键点：
- **问题新颖性**：wall-clock time vs token budget
- **评测全面性**：三种不同的时间消耗场景
- **发现意外性**：frontier models在时间约束下表现不佳

需要我调整篇幅或侧重点吗？

3.    
    
    ## LLM ColdStart
    
Before RL，我们首先蒸馏了一些数据以进行cold start以及评测。对general tasks，我们在AM-1.4M数据集的query上，通过为Qwen3-235B-2507-Instruct模型提供一个计数器，我们蒸馏了1M规模的数据。在这些数据中，模型在用时限制长短不一的general tasks上，均给出了准确的解答。这批数据被视作general task的cold start数据。
接下来，在jericho tasks上，我们同样蒸馏了DeepSeek-V3.2的解答trace。为了防止cold start模型完全背记训练数据，对每个game，我们仅蒸馏了前50步的trace，以确保后续的step是模型自己探索的结果。在Machine Learning tasks上我们并没有进行cold start。我们紧接着将cold start数据mix，并混合了RUC-DataLab/DataScience-Instruct-500K作为Cold Start数据，在不同的模型基座上进行SFT以作为RL的起始点。 翻译这一段。

```latex
\subsubsection{LLM Cold Start}

Before applying reinforcement learning, we first distill supervised data to provide a warm start and establish baselines.

\paragraph{General reasoning tasks.}
We distill 1M training examples from Qwen3-235B-2507-Instruct on queries sampled from the AM-1.4M dataset.
Crucially, we equip the teacher model with a timer tool, enabling it to produce solutions under varying time budgets.
The resulting traces demonstrate accurate reasoning across diverse time constraints, providing cold-start supervision for time-aware general reasoning.

\paragraph{Interactive game tasks.}
Similarly, we distill interaction traces from DeepSeek-V3.2 on Jericho environments.
To prevent memorization, we limit distillation to the first 50 steps per game, ensuring that subsequent exploration must be learned rather than merely recalled.

\paragraph{Machine learning tasks.}
We do not perform task-specific distillation for ML tasks, as their diversity and computational cost make exhaustive coverage impractical.

\paragraph{Training.}
We combine the distilled data with RUC-DataLab/DataScience-Instruct-500K to form the final cold-start mixture.
This dataset is used to fine-tune various base models via supervised learning, providing the initialization point for subsequent RL training.
```

**改进要点：**

1. **分段清晰**：用paragraph分别说明三类任务的处理
2. **突出设计意图**：
   - General tasks: "equip with timer tool" → time-aware
   - Interactive games: "limit to 50 steps" → prevent memorization
   - ML tasks: "diversity and cost" → explain why no distillation
3. **逻辑连贯**：最后统一说明如何组合数据和训练
4. **术语准确**：cold start → warm start (更标准), distill (保持一致性)

需要我调整细节或添加更多技术说明吗？

在提出Timely-Eval后，我们认为，awareness of time这一能力是应当，也是可以在训练中强化的。由于时延的浮动是动态且实时的反馈，因此Reinforcement Learning是能够较好地调节模型这一能力的。因而，我们设计了一个基于time的多轮交互强化学习算法Timely-RL。
首先，在RL过程中，模型在第i步交互获取的时间反馈 t(i) 由 模型推理时间 t_gen(i) 和 工具执行时间 t_tool(i) 求和得到。此时，t_gen(i) 的真实值是由训练时的硬件资源决定的。为了让模型能够适应不同条件下的推理，我们将t_gen(i) 乘以一个系数alpha作为模型获取的实际反馈，以模拟更快或更慢的设备上模型的实际推理情况。同时，我们将tool部署在一个硬件资源固定的服务器上，以确保工具调用反馈速度的稳定性。 接下来，我们为三个任务分别设计了对应的reward函数，并将三个任务混合进行RL训练。考虑任意一个response，假设其准确率reward为 r_acc， 任务完成耗时为 t， 用时限制为 T_max ，则三项任务的reward function 可以统一表述为:
$$\begin{equation} R(t, r_{\text{acc}}) = \begin{cases} 0, & \text{if } t > T_{\text{max}} \\ r_{\text{format}}, & \text{if } t \le T_{\text{max}} \land r_{\text{acc}} = 0 \\ r_{\text{format}} + r_{\text{acc}} + \lambda\cdot U(t, T_{\text{max}}), & \text{if } t \le T_{\text{max}} \land r_{\text{acc}} > 0 \end{cases} \end{equation}$$
其中 r_format 为格式准确且模型准时回答完成的固定奖励reward，$$ U(t, T_{\text{max}})$$ 为模型对时间开销的利用率，计算方法为:
$$\begin{equation} U(t) = \sin\left( \frac{\pi}{2} \cdot \min\left(\frac{t}{T_{\text{max}}}, 1\right) \right) \end{equation}$$
这个映射方法确保，随着时间比率接近 1.0，奖励逐渐平稳并最终饱和，以此减轻截止时间附近微小时间波动的影响，让训练更加稳定。 接下来这一个subsection

```latex
\subsection{Timely-RL: Reinforcement Learning for Time-Aware Reasoning}

Having established \textsc{Timely-Eval}, we ask: \textbf{can models learn to manage time through training?}
We argue that time awareness is both necessary and trainable.
Since time costs fluctuate dynamically based on real-time execution, reinforcement learning provides a natural framework for optimizing this behavior.
We thus propose \textsc{Timely-RL}, a multi-turn interactive RL algorithm designed to teach models to reason effectively under time constraints.

\subsubsection{Time Feedback Mechanism}

At each interaction step $i$, the model observes time feedback $t(i)$ decomposed into two components:
$$
t(i) = t_{\text{gen}}(i) + t_{\text{tool}}(i)
$$
where $t_{\text{gen}}(i)$ denotes generation latency and $t_{\text{tool}}(i)$ denotes tool execution time.

A key challenge is hardware variability: $t_{\text{gen}}(i)$ depends heavily on the training infrastructure.
To ensure models generalize across deployment conditions, we introduce a scaling factor $\alpha$ applied to observed generation time:
$$
t_{\text{gen}}^{\text{obs}}(i) = \alpha \cdot t_{\text{gen}}(i)
$$
This simulates faster ($\alpha < 1$) or slower ($\alpha > 1$) devices during training.
Meanwhile, we deploy tools on fixed-resource servers to stabilize $t_{\text{tool}}(i)$ and isolate the model's reasoning behavior from environment noise.

\subsubsection{Unified Reward Function}

We design a unified reward function across all three task categories.
For any response with accuracy reward $r_{\text{acc}}$, completion time $t$, and budget $T_{\text{max}}$, the reward is:
$$
R(t, r_{\text{acc}}) = 
\begin{cases} 
0, & \text{if } t > T_{\text{max}} \\[6pt]
r_{\text{format}}, & \text{if } t \le T_{\text{max}} \land r_{\text{acc}} = 0 \\[6pt]
r_{\text{format}} + r_{\text{acc}} + \lambda \cdot U(t, T_{\text{max}}), & \text{if } t \le T_{\text{max}} \land r_{\text{acc}} > 0
\end{cases}
$$
where $r_{\text{format}}$ is a fixed bonus for valid, timely responses, and $U(t, T_{\text{max}})$ measures time utilization:
$$
U(t, T_{\text{max}}) = \sin\left( \frac{\pi}{2} \cdot \min\left(\frac{t}{T_{\text{max}}}, 1\right) \right)
$$

This design serves multiple purposes.
First, the timeout penalty ($R = 0$ when $t > T_{\text{max}}$) enforces hard deadlines.
Second, $r_{\text{format}}$ rewards timely completion regardless of correctness, encouraging models to respect budgets.
Third, the utilization term $U(t, T_{\text{max}})$ incentivizes using available time effectively---rewarding models that approach (but do not exceed) the deadline.

The sinusoidal mapping ensures smooth saturation near $t = T_{\text{max}}$, reducing sensitivity to minor timing fluctuations and stabilizing training.
Intuitively, a model that finishes at 99\% of the budget should not be penalized much compared to one finishing at 100\%, but a model finishing at 50\% leaves value on the table.
The hyperparameter $\lambda$ controls the trade-off between accuracy and time utilization.
```

**改进要点：**

1. **引入段落清晰**：用问题引出动机（"can models learn to manage time?"）

2. **技术细节精确**：
   - 公式推导完整
   - $\alpha$ 的作用解释清楚
   - 三段式reward的设计意图逐一说明

3. **直觉解释**：
   - "simulates faster/slower devices"
   - "smooth saturation near deadline"
   - "finishing at 50% leaves value on the table"

4. **层次分明**：分subsubsection讨论time feedback和reward design
